topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt too bright, a harsh, unforgiving glare that seemed to strip the color from everything. Elias stood near the entrance, his hands clenched so tightly his knuckles were white islands against his skin. He scanned the room, a slow, painful sweep of his gaze, searching for the familiar, defiant splash of cerulean blue that was his signature. Instead, there it was—his canvas, draped across a pedestal, illuminated like a misplaced artifact. A knot tightened in his chest, a sudden, cold pressure that made his breath catch. He took a hesitant step forward, the polished floor echoing his tremor. When a woman with a knowing, detached smile approached, her voice smooth as oiled silk, "A truly captivating piece, wouldn't you agree?" The sound of her approval felt like a physical blow, leaving him rooted to the spot, unable to form a coherent response.

[story 2]

I kept my eyes fixed on the exit sign, counting the seconds until I could dissolve back into the anonymity of the street. The air in the exhibition hall was thick with hushed admiration, a palpable weight pressing down on my shoulders. Every glance felt like an interrogation, dissecting the exposed parts of me. My throat felt suddenly dry, a tight band constricting my ability to speak. Then I saw it—my latest sculpture, standing center stage, rendered in bronze, impossibly perfect. A wave of intense shame washed over me, making my skin prickle with heat. Someone, or some committee, had decided my expression was worth more than my right to privacy. I turned away sharply, the movement jerky, and walked toward the shadows, feeling every eye on my retreating form, a thousand unseen needles pricking at my composure.

[story 3]

The silence in the space was deafening, broken only by the faint rustle of expensive fabric. I huddled near a pillar, pulling my jacket tighter around myself as if the chill radiating from the marble could seep into my bones. My gaze kept darting toward the artwork—my intricate watercolor series, displayed with a bold, almost aggressive placement. A tremor started low in my stomach, a disconcerting flutter that made my knees feel weak. I watched a group of patrons circle the piece, their murmurs sounding like judgments echoing in a vast, empty chamber. When one man paused directly in front of the painting, his expression unreadable, I felt a desperate urge to vanish, to become invisible. I squeezed my eyes shut for a brief second, trying to physically contain the sudden rush of exposed feeling.
